questions.explanation: Escapability ensures that users are never 'trapped' in a part of the system. It is often implemented via 'Cancel' buttons or the 'Esc' key. This reduces user anxiety and supports exploration. Option B is the correct design principle.

questions.question: In Lecture 36, 'Usability Evaluation' is categorized into two main types based on when they occur. What is the primary purpose of 'Formative Evaluation'?
options: To provide a final grade or score for the software after release.
To identify usability problems and influence the design while the product is still being developed.
To compare two finished products to see which one performs better.
To calculate the total return on investment (ROI) for the development team.
questions.explanation: Formative evaluation is conducted during the design process to 'form' or improve the design. It is diagnostic in nature, helping designers identify what isn't working while there is still time to change it. Summative evaluation (Option A), conversely, happens at the end to assess the finished product. Option B is the correct HCI definition.

questions.question: In Lecture 36, the 'Cognitive Walkthrough' is described as an 'Analytical' evaluation method. What distinguishes an analytical method from an empirical one?
options: Analytical methods require a high-speed computer for simulation.
Analytical methods involve experts or designers simulating the user's thought process without necessarily involving end-users.
Analytical methods are performed only after the product is released to the market.
Analytical methods rely entirely on measuring the user's heart rate and eye movements.
questions.explanation: Analytical evaluation (like Heuristic Evaluation or Cognitive Walkthrough) is based on the expert's knowledge and theories of human cognition. Empirical evaluation involves observing 'real' users. Option B is the correct distinction as per the handouts.

questions.question: According to Jakob Nielsen's Heuristics (Lecture 37), the principle of 'Consistency and Standards' implies that:
options: Users should never be allowed to make mistakes.
The system should always use the latest, most expensive hardware standards.
Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing.
Every page in a website must have a completely unique layout to keep users interested.
questions.explanation: Consistency and Standards means following platform conventions and being internaly consistent. For example, if 'Quit' means closing the app in one menu, it shouldn't mean 'Delete' in another. Users rely on these standards to predict system behavior. Option C is the core of this heuristic.

questions.question: In a 'Heuristic Evaluation' (Lecture 37), what is the recommended number of evaluators to find the majority of usability problems?
options: A single expert is enough for any system.
Between 3 to 5 evaluators.
At least 50 representative end-users.
One developer from every department.
questions.explanation: Nielsen's research shows that a single evaluator only finds about 35% of usability problems. However, as you add more evaluators, the number of new problems found diminishes. The 'sweet spot' for cost-effectiveness is generally 3 to 5 evaluators, who can collectively find about 75-80% of issues. Option B is the industry standard.

questions.question: In the context of 'Heuristic Evaluation,' what does the heuristic 'Error Prevention' suggest?
options: Writing very detailed error messages so users know exactly what they did wrong.
Designing the system so that the user is less likely to make an error in the first place.
Preventing the user from using the software if they haven't read the manual.
Automatically fixing every typo the user makes without telling them.
questions.explanation: While good error messages are important, a better design prevents the problem from occurring at all (e.g., using a date-picker instead of a text box to prevent date format errors). Option B follows the principle that 'prevention is better than cure' in HCI.

questions.question: According to Jakob Nielsen's Heuristics (Lecture 37), the principle of 'Match between system and the real world' suggests that:
options: The system should be as expensive as real-world professional tools.
The system should speak the users' language, using words, phrases, and concepts familiar to the user rather than system-oriented terms.
The system must be able to simulate physical gravity and lighting.
The system should automatically translate all technical jargon into Latin.
questions.explanation: This heuristic focuses on the 'Mental Model.' Information should appear in a natural and logical order, using real-world conventions (e.g., using a folder icon for a directory). Option B is the correct interpretation.

questions.question: In a 'Heuristic Evaluation' (Lecture 37), why is it recommended that evaluators work independently before communicating their findings?
options: To prevent them from arguing and wasting time.
To ensure that the evaluations are unbiased and independent, maximizing the variety of problems found.
Because the software can only be used by one person at a time.
To save on the costs of renting a shared meeting room.
questions.explanation: If evaluators work together, they might influence each other's opinions (groupthink). By working alone, they are more likely to find a broader range of unique usability issues. Option B is the standard procedural requirement.

questions.question: Which Nielsen Heuristic (Lecture 37) is violated if a 'Delete' button and a 'Save' button look identical and are placed right next to each other?
options: Visibility of system status
Error prevention
Flexibility and efficiency of use
Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors
questions.explanation: Even better than good error messages is a design which prevents a problem from occurring in the first place. Placing high-risk actions (Delete) next to common actions (Save) without visual distinction is a failure of 'Error Prevention.' Option B is the correct heuristic.

questions.question: Which evaluation method is most appropriate for finding 'minor' usability problems that occur infrequently (Lecture 37)?
options: Cognitive Walkthrough
Heuristic Evaluation
Questionnaires
Interviews
questions.explanation: Because Heuristic Evaluation involves experts systematically checking every part of the interface against a list of rules, it is very good at finding 'small' or aesthetic issues that a casual user might ignore during a timed task. Option B is the most thorough for this purpose.

questions.question: Which evaluation method involves an expert 'walking through' a specific task while asking four questions about the user's psychological state at each step?
options: Heuristic Evaluation
Cognitive Walkthrough
Pluralistic Walkthrough
Model-Based Evaluation
questions.explanation: Cognitive Walkthrough is a theory-based evaluation method (Lecture 38). It focuses on how easy it is for new users to learn a system by exploring. The evaluator examines each step of a task to see if the user will notice the correct control, interpret it correctly, and see that they are making progress. Option B is the correct method.

questions.question: Lecture 38 discusses 'Cognitive Walkthrough.' What is the 'Success Story' the evaluator looks for at each step of the task?
options: The user finds the correct action and interprets the system's feedback correctly to see that they are moving toward their goal.
The user manages to finish the task without looking at the screen.
The system does not crash during the entire evaluation process.
The evaluator is able to guess the user's password.
questions.explanation: A success story occurs when the user's mental model aligns with the system design. The evaluator checks if the user will notice the correct control, recognize it is the right one, and understand the feedback. Option A is the correct answer.

questions.question: In Lecture 39, 'Observational Evaluation' techniques include 'Think Aloud.' What is a major advantage of this technique?
options: It allows the user to perform the task much faster than usual.
It provides direct insight into the user's mental model and thought processes during the task.
It requires no special equipment or recording devices.
It is the best method for testing the physical durability of the keyboard.
questions.explanation: In 'Think Aloud,' users are asked to verbalize their thoughts, feelings, and intentions as they use the system. This reveals why they are making certain choices and where their mental model differs from the designer's. Option B is the primary benefit of this qualitative method.

questions.question: In Lecture 39, 'Indirect Observation' techniques include 'Interaction Logging.' What does this involve?
options: The evaluator sitting next to the user and taking handwritten notes.
The system automatically recording the user's key presses and mouse clicks for later analysis.
Interviewing the user's supervisor to see how they perform.
Recording the user's face with a video camera during the test.
questions.explanation: Interaction logging (or data logging) provides quantitative data on how users navigate the system, where they pause, and what errors they frequently trigger. It is 'indirect' because the evaluator does not need to be physically present. Option B is the correct method.

questions.question: In the 'Think Aloud' protocol (Lecture 39), what should the evaluator do if the user stops talking for a long period?
options: End the test immediately.
Give the user the answer to the current problem.
Gently prompt the user to keep talking (e.g., 'What are you thinking now?').
Leave the room until the user is ready to continue.
questions.explanation: The evaluator's role in Think Aloud is to keep the stream of consciousness flowing without leading the user toward a specific solution. Constant gentle prompting is a standard part of the protocol. Option C is the correct action.

questions.question: What is the 'Decoy Effect' (or 'Cooperative Evaluation') as mentioned in the handouts regarding user testing?
options: A technique where the evaluator pretends to be a user to trick the system.
A process where the user and the evaluator talk to each other to clarify problems during the test.
Using fake software versions to see if users can spot the difference.
Providing users with incorrect instructions to see how they recover.
questions.explanation: Cooperative Evaluation is a variation of 'Think Aloud' where the user and the evaluator are encouraged to collaborate. If the user gets stuck, the evaluator can ask questions or provide hints, making it a less formal but highly informative 'partnership' in finding bugs. Option B is the correct description.

questions.question: According to Lecture 40, what is the 'Severity Rating' in a usability report used for?
options: To determine which developer should be fired for making the error.
To prioritize which usability problems should be fixed first based on their impact.
To calculate the total number of lines of code in the system.
To measure the physical brightness of the screen.
questions.explanation: Not all usability problems are equal. A typo is a low-severity issue, while a button that accidentally deletes all data is high-severity. Severity ratings help management allocate limited resources to fix the most 'damaging' problems first. Option B is the correct use case.

questions.question: Which of the following is a 'Quantitative' measure of usability often collected during a summative evaluation?
options: A user's verbal comment about liking the color blue.
The average time taken to complete a specific task (Task Completion Time).
A description of the user's facial expression when frustrated.
The evaluator's notes on the user's general behavior.
questions.explanation: Quantitative data is numerical and measurable. Task completion times, error rates, and the number of clicks are all objective metrics. Qualitative data (Options A, C, D) describes the 'quality' or nature of the experience. Option B is a classic quantitative usability metric.

questions.question: In Lecture 38, 'Query Techniques' include 'Questionnaires' and 'Interviews.' When is a Questionnaire generally preferred over an Interview?
options: When you need deep, complex, and open-ended feedback from a single expert.
When you need to collect data from a large number of users quickly and cheaply.
When the user does not know how to read or write.
When you want to observe the user's physical interaction with the mouse.
questions.explanation: Questionnaires are excellent for reaching a wide audience and gathering statistically significant data at a low cost. Interviews are better for 'deep dives' but are time-consuming. Option B is the correct justification for using questionnaires.

questions.question: According to the principles of 'Universal Design' discussed in Lecture 41, what is the core philosophy of this approach?
options: Designing specific versions of a product for every possible disability.
Designing products so they are usable by as many people as possible without the need for adaptation.
Ensuring that all software is translated into every major world language.
Making technology so cheap that everyone in the world can afford it.
questions.explanation: Universal Design (or Design for All) is the process of creating products that are accessible and usable by people with the widest possible range of abilities and in the widest possible range of situations. It moves away from 'special' designs for the disabled and toward one inclusive design. Option B is the correct academic definition.

questions.question: The principle of 'Simple and Intuitive Use' in Universal Design (Lecture 41) suggests that the design should be easy to understand regardless of what factor?
options: The user's physical strength or stamina.
The user's experience, knowledge, language skills, or current concentration level.
The cost of the computer hardware being used.
The speed of the user's internet connection.
questions.explanation: This principle aims to eliminate unnecessary complexity. A design should be intuitive so that a wide range of users—from novices to experts and those with varying literacy levels—can use it without struggle.

questions.question: Under Universal Design (Lecture 41), providing 'Tolerance for Error' means the design should:
options: Prevent users from ever making a mistake.
Minimize hazards and the adverse consequences of accidental or unintended actions.
Automatically fix all user typos without any notification.
Charge a fee every time the user clicks the wrong button.
questions.explanation: This principle involves arranging elements to minimize hazards (e.g., putting the 'Delete' button away from 'Save') and providing 'Undo' functions for recovery.

questions.question: In Lecture 42, 'Help and Documentation' are classified into different types. What is 'Context-Sensitive Help'?
options: A printed manual that comes in the software box.
Help that automatically provides information about the specific task or UI element the user is currently interacting with.
A searchable index of every command available in the software.
A community forum where users help each other solve problems.
questions.explanation: Context-sensitive help reduces the user's search time by providing immediate, relevant information based on what they are doing (e.g., 'Tooltips' or 'What's This?' help). It eliminates the need for the user to leave their current context to look through a general manual. Option B is the correct description.

questions.question: According to Lecture 42, why is 'Minimalism' important in documentation?
options: To save money on paper and printing costs.
To ensure users aren't overwhelmed by too much information and can find answers quickly.
Because users today have shorter attention spans than in the past.
To allow more room on the screen for advertisements.
questions.explanation: The 'Minimalist' approach to documentation (proposed by Carroll) suggests that users are task-oriented and don't want to read long manuals. Documentation should be brief, focused on real tasks, and support 'learning by doing' and error recovery. Option B is the correct HCI rationale.

questions.question: In Lecture 42, which type of documentation is intended to help users learn the basics of a system through a structured, step-by-step approach?
options: Reference Manuals
Tutorials
Quick Reference Guides
Searchable Indexes
questions.explanation: Tutorials are designed for new users. They guide the user through specific tasks to build foundational knowledge, whereas reference manuals are for experts looking up specific details.

questions.question: In Lecture 42, which documentation style focuses on 'Learning by Doing' and uses real-world tasks rather than abstract feature descriptions?
options: System-centered documentation
Minimalist documentation
Comprehensive Reference Manuals
Command Summary Sheets
questions.explanation: Minimalism (proposed by John Carroll) assumes users want to get to work immediately. It provides short, task-oriented instructions that encourage active exploration and error recovery.

questions.question: According to Lecture 43, what is the primary focus of 'Computer-Supported Cooperative Work' (CSCW)?
options: Improving the speed of internet connections in office buildings.
Studying how technology can support and enhance the way people work together in groups.
Developing better anti-virus software for corporate networks.
Training employees on how to use basic office productivity software.
questions.explanation: CSCW (often implemented as Groupware) focuses on the social and collaborative aspects of work. It examines how people use technology to communicate, coordinate, and collaborate, whether they are in the same room or across the world. Option B identifies the core area of study for CSCW.

questions.question: What is the 'Social Translucence' concept mentioned in the context of CSCW (Lecture 43)?
options: Making the screen transparent so users can see each other through it.
Designing systems that make the presence and activities of other group members visible to everyone.
Ensuring that all social media data is encrypted for privacy.
A method for hiding the identity of users in a group chat.
questions.explanation: Social translucence is about making 'social cues' visible in a digital environment. If I can see that a colleague is 'typing' or 'busy' in a groupware tool, I can better coordinate my interaction with them. It brings the awareness of a shared physical office into the digital space. Option B is the correct application.

questions.question: In the context of 'Groupware' (Lecture 43), what is 'Floor Control'?
options: A physical safety regulation for computer laboratories.
A mechanism that manages who has the right to speak or input data in a shared session.
The process of cleaning the physical office space.
A way to monitor the electricity usage of a server room.
questions.explanation: In shared applications, 'floor control' (or concurrency control) ensures that users don't overwrite each other's work. It determines who has the 'turn' to edit a document or move an object in a synchronous session. Option B is the correct groupware term.

questions.question: In the 'Time/Space Matrix' for groupware (Lecture 43), a 'Synchronous, Remote' interaction is best exemplified by:
options: A face-to-face meeting in a physical conference room.
A shared digital whiteboard used by two people in different cities at the same time.
An email thread where people reply whenever they are free.
A shared folder on a server where files are stored for later use.
questions.explanation: Synchronous means 'at the same time,' and Remote means 'in different places.' A video conference or a shared real-time whiteboard allows people in different locations to interact simultaneously. Option B fits both criteria.

questions.question: Lecture 44 discusses 'Multi-Modal' interfaces. What does this term refer to?
options: Interfaces that can run on multiple different operating systems.
Interfaces that use more than one human input/output channel simultaneously (e.g., speech and gesture).
Software that has both a 'Free' and a 'Premium' version.
Computers that can be used by more than one person at a time.
questions.explanation: Multi-modal interaction provides the user with multiple channels of communication, such as sight, sound, and touch. For example, a system might allow a user to point at a screen while saying 'Put that there.' This mimics natural human-to-human communication. Option B is the correct definition.

questions.question: In the context of 'Multi-modal' interaction (Lecture 44), what is 'Haptic' feedback?
options: Feedback provided through spoken audio.
Feedback provided through the sense of touch, such as vibrations or resistance.
Feedback provided through high-resolution graphics.
Feedback provided through changes in the user's heart rate.
questions.explanation: Haptic interaction involves touch and physical sensation. Examples include the vibration of a phone or the 'force feedback' in a gaming steering wheel.

questions.question: According to Lecture 44, 'Speech Recognition' in HCI faces which major challenge in noisy environments?
options: The computer running out of battery too quickly.
The 'Cocktail Party Effect' (difficulty in isolating the user's voice from background noise).
The user forgetting their own password.
The system being unable to store large audio files.
questions.explanation: Isolating a specific voice from ambient noise or other speakers remains a significant technical hurdle for robust speech-based interfaces.

questions.question: In 'Universal Design,' the principle of 'Equitable Use' means:
options: The design is useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities.
The design is only available to those who pay an equal share of the cost.
The design uses symbols that are equally understood by all cultures.
The software provides equal processing power to all connected users.
questions.explanation: Equitable Use is the first principle of Universal Design. It states that the design should provide the same means of use for all users: identical whenever possible, equivalent when not. It avoids segregating or stigmatizing any users. Option A is the correct core meaning.

questions.question: Which specific component of the 'Model Human Processor' (Lecture 6) is characterized by having the shortest decay time for information?
options: Long-term Memory
Short-term (Working) Memory
Sensory Image Store (Visual/Auditory)
The Motor Processor
questions.explanation: The Sensory Image Stores (Visual Image Store and Auditory Image Store) act as buffers for incoming raw data. Information in these stores decays extremely rapidly (often within milliseconds) unless it is moved to Working Memory for processing. Option C is the correct answer regarding temporal decay.

questions.question: According to the 'Model Human Processor' (Lecture 6), which processor would be most active when a user decides to click a button after seeing a prompt?
options: The Perceptual Processor only.
The Perceptual, Cognitive, and Motor Processors in sequence.
The Motor Processor only.
The Cognitive and Perceptual Processors simultaneously.
questions.explanation: The MHP model suggests a pipeline: the Perceptual system 'sees' the prompt, the Cognitive system 'decides' to click, and the Motor system 'executes' the hand movement. They work in a serial sequence to complete the interaction. Option B is the correct sequence of operations.

questions.question: Under the 'Principles of Color' in Lecture 8, what is 'Simultaneous Contrast'?
options: The ability of a screen to show 16 million colors at once.
The phenomenon where the perception of a color is changed by the color surrounding it.
A technique for compressing image files to save storage space.
The use of black and white text to ensure maximum readability.
questions.explanation: Simultaneous contrast occurs because the eye's receptors react to the boundaries between colors. A gray square will look lighter on a black background and darker on a white background. Designers must be aware of this to ensure that interface elements remain legible regardless of their surrounding environment. Option B is the correct definition.

questions.question: In Lecture 9, 'Forgetting' from Long-term Memory is primarily attributed to which two processes?
options: Deletion and Overwriting.
Decay and Interference.
Format Incompatibility and Disk Corruption.
Lack of interest and physical tiredness.
questions.explanation: Psychologists suggest that information in LTM may not actually be deleted, but rather becomes inaccessible. 'Decay' suggests the trace weakens over time if not retrieved. 'Interference' suggests new information or old information makes it difficult to find the desired memory. Option B represents the academic consensus in the handouts.

questions.question: Regarding 'Visual Perception' (Lecture 7), why is it difficult for humans to distinguish between small blue objects compared to red or green ones?
options: Blue light has a longer wavelength that causes blurriness.
The central part of the fovea is almost 'blue-blind' due to a lack of blue-sensitive cones.
Blue is a 'cool' color that the brain naturally ignores to save energy.
The eye's lens cannot focus on blue light correctly.
questions.explanation: The fovea, which is the area of highest visual acuity, is densely packed with red and green cones, but blue cones are much more sparse and almost non-existent at the very center. This makes high-detail work in blue (like small blue text) very difficult for the human visual system. Option B is the correct physiological reason.

questions.question: What is the 'Execution-Evaluation Cycle' as discussed in Lecture 11 (referenced as foundational in early lectures)?
options: The process of writing code and then running it to check for bugs.
A model describing the steps a user takes to interact with a system and assess the results.
A financial cycle used to determine the profitability of a software company.
A hardware test that ensures the CPU is functioning at the correct clock speed.
questions.explanation: Donald Norman's cycle describes how a user starts with a goal, executes actions on the system (Execution), and then checks the system's output to see if the goal was achieved (Evaluation). This cycle is used to identify where interaction 'breakdowns' occur. Option B is the correct HCI definition.

questions.question: In Donald Norman's Seven Stages of Action (Lecture 11), the 'Gulf of Evaluation' is narrowed when the system provides which of the following?
options: A faster processor to decrease calculation time.
Clear, immediate, and interpretable feedback about the system state.
A more complex set of features for the expert user.
A physical manual with at least 100 pages of documentation.
questions.explanation: The Gulf of Evaluation reflects the amount of effort the user must exert to interpret the physical state of the system and determine how well their expectations have been met. High-quality feedback that matches the user's mental model helps bridge this gap. Option B is the correct design strategy for reducing this gulf.

questions.question: In the context of Norman’s Seven Stages of Action (Lecture 11), what is the 'Gulf of Execution'?
options: The difficulty users face in determining the current state of the system.
The gap between the user's intentions and the allowable actions provided by the system.
The time it takes for the CPU to process a user's request.
The distance between the user's physical location and the computer terminal.
questions.explanation: The Gulf of Execution measures how much effort the user must expend to figure out how to operate the system to achieve their goal. If the interface does not clearly show which buttons to press or how to move the mouse to achieve an intention, the Gulf of Execution is wide. Option B is the correct academic definition.

questions.question: In Lecture 10, 'Problem Solving' is described as a process of 'State-Space Search.' What does the 'Initial State' represent?
options: The system after the user has successfully finished the task.
The condition of the system at the moment the user decides to start a task.
The factory settings of the computer hardware.
The first line of code in the application's main function.
questions.explanation: Problem solving is viewed as moving from an 'Initial State' (where the problem exists) to a 'Goal State' (where the problem is solved) by applying operators (actions). Understanding these states helps designers build better navigation. Option B is the correct definition.

questions.question: In Lecture 5, 'Efficiency' as a usability goal is defined as:
options: How quickly a user can perform a task after they have learned the system.
How long it takes a user to learn the system from scratch.
The number of features the software provides for the price.
The amount of RAM the software uses during operation.
questions.explanation: While 'Learnability' measures how easy it is to start, 'Efficiency' measures the productivity of the user once they are familiar with the system (e.g., using shortcuts or expert modes). Option A is the correct usability metric definition.

questions.question: Which usability goal from Lecture 5 focuses on how easy it is for a user to 're-establish' their proficiency after not using the system for a long time?
options: Learnability
Memorability
Efficiency
Safety
questions.explanation: Memorability refers to the ability of a casual user to return to a system and remember how to use it without having to relearn everything from scratch. This is vital for systems used infrequently, like tax software. Option B is the correct goal.

questions.question: What does the 'Safety' usability goal (Lecture 5) primarily aim to prevent?
options: The computer from being stolen from the office.
Users from making serious errors and providing a way to recover if they do.
Unauthorized hackers from accessing the system.
The monitor from emitting too much blue light.
questions.explanation: Safety in usability means protecting the user from dangerous conditions and preventing them from making irreversible mistakes (e.g., a 'Confirm Delete' dialog). It is about 'Error Tolerance.' Option B is the correct HCI definition.

questions.question: Usability is often defined by five components (ISO 9241). Which of these is NOT one of the core usability goals discussed in Lecture 5?
options: Subjective Satisfaction
Learnability
Profitability
Error Rate
questions.explanation: The core usability goals are Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Errors (Safety), and Satisfaction. While Profitability may be a business goal, it is not a direct 'usability' metric. Option C is the correct answer.

questions.question: In the context of 'Display Devices' (Lecture 14), what does the term 'Pitch' (or Dot Pitch) refer to?
options: The angle at which a user must look at the screen for best clarity.
The distance between the centers of two adjacent pixels of the same color.
The speed at which the electron beam scans the screen.
The audio frequency emitted by the monitor's internal speakers.
questions.explanation: Dot pitch is a measure of image clarity. A smaller pitch means the pixels are closer together, resulting in a sharper image. It is a critical hardware specification for high-resolution displays. Option B is the correct technical definition.

questions.question: What is the 'Refresh Rate' of a monitor (Lecture 14), and why is it important for the user?
options: The time it takes for a monitor to turn on; important for productivity.
The number of times per second the screen image is redrawn; important to prevent flicker and eye strain.
The speed of the data transfer between the PC and the monitor.
The frequency at which the monitor needs to be cleaned.
questions.explanation: If the refresh rate is too low (below 60-72Hz for CRTs), the human eye perceives flicker, which leads to headaches and fatigue. High refresh rates ensure a stable, comfortable image for the user. Option B is the correct definition.

questions.question: According to Lecture 14, what is the primary disadvantage of 'CRT' monitors compared to 'LCD' monitors?
options: CRTs have a much narrower viewing angle.
CRTs are bulky, heavy, and consume significantly more power.
CRTs cannot display more than 256 colors.
CRTs have a much slower response time for fast-moving images.
questions.explanation: While CRTs once offered better color and response times, their physical footprint (depth and weight) and high energy consumption made them less practical than the thin-film transistor (TFT) LCDs. Option B correctly identifies the main hardware drawback.

questions.question: Lecture 15 discusses 'Non-speech sound'. What is the difference between 'Auditory Icons' and 'Earcons'?
options: Auditory Icons are music; Earcons are spoken words.
Auditory Icons use natural, everyday sounds; Earcons use abstract, synthetic musical tones.
Auditory Icons are loud; Earcons are quiet.
Auditory Icons are for blind users; Earcons are for deaf users.
questions.explanation: Auditory Icons use natural analogies (e.g., the sound of a closing door for 'Close Window'). Earcons use structured, abstract patterns of notes to represent actions, which must be learned as they have no natural meaning. Option B is the correct distinction.

questions.question: In the context of 'Display Devices' (Lecture 14/16), what is the 'Aspect Ratio' of a screen?
options: The ratio of the number of red pixels to the number of blue pixels.
The ratio of the screen's width to its height.
The ratio of the power consumption to the brightness output.
The ratio of the screen's physical size to its weight.
questions.explanation: Aspect ratio describes the proportional relationship between the width and height of a display. Standard monitors were traditionally 4:3, while modern widescreen displays are typically 16:9 or 16:10. This is a critical specification for ensuring interface layouts do not appear stretched. Option B is the correct definition.

questions.question: What is the 'Active Matrix' technology in LCD monitors primarily designed to improve?
questions.explanation: Active Matrix (TFT) uses a transistor for each pixel, which allows for faster response times and higher contrast compared to older Passive Matrix displays. This reduces 'ghosting' or blurring of moving images. Option B is the correct technical advantage.
options: The physical weight and thickness of the monitor.
The refresh speed and clarity of the image compared to passive matrix.
The amount of electricity the monitor consumes from the wall outlet.
The ability of the monitor to be viewed from extreme side angles.

questions.question: Which display technology mentioned in the handouts is described as being 'Energy Efficient' because it does not require a backlight to illuminate individual pixels?
options: Standard LCD
OLED (Organic Light Emitting Diode)
CRT
Plasma
questions.explanation: OLED pixels emit their own light, meaning they can be turned off completely to show 'true black,' which saves energy compared to LCDs that require a constant backlight (which stays on even for black pixels). Option B is the correct technology.

questions.question: In Lecture 16, 'Random Access' memory in the context of display buffers is discussed. What is the role of the 'Video RAM' (VRAM)?
options: To store the permanent operating system files.
To hold the digital representation of the screen image before it is displayed.
To increase the physical speed of the mouse cursor.
To prevent the monitor from overheating.
questions.explanation: The Video RAM or Frame Buffer stores the color/intensity value of every pixel on the screen. The display controller reads this memory to refresh the monitor image. Option B is the correct functional definition.

questions.question: Which display technology (Lecture 16) works by sandwiching a liquid crystal solution between two glass plates and using an electric current to align the crystals?
options: CRT (Cathode Ray Tube)
LCD (Liquid Crystal Display)
Plasma Display
LED (Light Emitting Diode)
questions.explanation: LCDs do not emit light directly; they use the light-modulating properties of liquid crystals. When a voltage is applied, the crystals untwist to block or allow light from a backlight to pass through. Option B is the correct technology.

questions.question: In Lecture 20, the concept of 'Information Density' in screen design suggests that:
options: Every pixel on the screen should contain meaningful data.
The designer should balance the amount of information to avoid overwhelming the user while providing enough context.
High-density screens are always more usable than low-density screens.
Users prefer screens that are completely filled with text rather than images.
questions.explanation: Information density is a trade-off. Too little information leads to excessive navigation (many pages); too much information leads to visual clutter and search difficulty. A usable design finds the 'middle ground' for the specific user task. Option B is the correct design principle.

questions.question: According to Lecture 20, why is 'Contrast' critical in screen design?
options: It makes the computer look more modern.
It ensures that text is legible against its background and that important elements stand out.
It reduces the amount of electricity the monitor uses.
It allows the software to be translated into different languages easily.
questions.explanation: Contrast (especially luminance contrast) is the most important factor for readability. Black text on a white background provides the highest contrast. Poor contrast leads to eye strain and errors. Option B is the correct design rationale.

questions.question: What is the 'Response Time' guideline for highly interactive tasks as mentioned in the handouts (Lecture 20)?
options: Response time should always be exactly 5 seconds.
Response time should be as close to instantaneous (under 0.1 seconds) as possible for frequent actions.
Users prefer a delay of at least 10 seconds to think about their next move.
Response time only matters for video games, not professional software.
questions.explanation: For a system to feel 'responsive' and maintain the user's flow, feedback should occur within 0.1 to 1.0 seconds. Delays beyond 10 seconds usually cause users to lose focus or think the system has crashed. Option B is the correct usability standard.

questions.question: In Lecture 29, the process of 'Data Analysis' for requirements involves creating 'Scenarios.' A scenario is best defined as:
options: A technical document listing the minimum RAM and CPU requirements.
A personalized story about a user performing a specific task in a specific context.
A list of all the software bugs found during the testing phase.
A flowchart showing the internal logic of the database.
questions.explanation: Scenarios are informal narrative descriptions. They help the design team humanize the requirements by describing how 'User X' would use the 'System Y' to achieve 'Goal Z' while facing specific real-world constraints. Option B is the correct HCI definition.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of the 'Conceptual Design' phase in the HCI lifecycle (Lecture 26)?
options: To write the final code in C++ or Java.
To produce a high-level model of the system's functionality and the user's mental model.
To determine the final retail price of the software.
To hire the marketing team for the product launch.
questions.explanation: Conceptual design is about the 'big picture.' It defines what the system will do, how it will behave, and what metaphors will be used (e.g., a 'shopping cart'). It happens before the physical design (colors, buttons, layout). Option B is the correct stage goal.

questions.question: Which of the following is a 'Non-functional' requirement for a mobile banking app?
options: The user must be able to transfer money between accounts.
The system must load the account balance within 2 seconds.
The user must be able to change their login password.
The system must generate a PDF receipt for every transaction.
questions.explanation: Functional requirements (A, C, D) describe specific actions the system performs. Non-functional requirements describe quality attributes or constraints. 'Performance' (loading within 2 seconds) is a classic non-functional requirement. Option B is the correct answer.

questions.question: According to Lecture 27, 'Functional Requirements' define:
options: How well the system performs its tasks (e.g., speed and reliability).
What the system should do (the specific services and functions provided).
The physical environment where the system will be used.
The budget and timeline for the software project.
questions.explanation: Functional requirements describe the interactions between the system and its environment, independent of its implementation. They define the 'what,' whereas non-functional requirements define the 'how well.'

questions.question: Lecture 29 discusses 'Scenarios' in the design process. What is a key benefit of using scenarios?
options: They provide a formal mathematical proof of the system's correctness.
They help designers think about the user's goals and tasks in a real-world, human-centric context.
They are used to calculate the exact lines of code needed.
They serve as a legal contract between the developer and the client.
questions.explanation: Scenarios are narrative descriptions of a user's activities. They allow designers to visualize how a product will be used in daily life, helping to identify missing requirements or potential usability hurdles.

questions.question: During 'Requirement Elicitation' (Lecture 27), why might a designer use 'Ethnography' (Observation)?
options: To save money on hiring professional testers.
To understand the actual work practices and social context that users might not be able to articulate in an interview.
To check the software for security vulnerabilities.
To record the user's keystrokes for performance metrics.
questions.explanation: Users often do things automatically and forget to mention them in interviews. By observing them in their natural environment, designers can see the 'real' workflow and hidden constraints.

questions.question: What is 'Usability Engineering' as described in Lecture 36?
options: The process of repairing broken computer hardware.
A disciplined, iterative approach to product development with a central focus on usability goals and metrics.
The study of how to make computers process data faster.
A branch of civil engineering that builds ergonomic office chairs.
questions.explanation: Usability Engineering (UE) involves specifying measurable usability goals early in the process and testing against them iteratively. It treats 'ease of use' as a technical requirement. Option B is the correct definition.

questions.question: According to Lecture 40, a 'Usability Lab' typically consists of two rooms separated by:
options: A soundproof concrete wall with no visibility.
A one-way mirror (half-silvered mirror).
A curtain that can be opened by the user.
A high-speed fiber optic cable.
questions.explanation: A one-way mirror allows the evaluators to observe the user's behavior and facial expressions without the user being distracted or intimidated by the presence of the observers. Option B is the standard lab setup.

questions.question: According to the 'Time/Space Matrix' (Lecture 43), an Asynchronous/Co-located interaction occurs when:
options: People work together at the same time in the same room.
People work in the same place but at different times (e.g., using a shared workstation).
People work in different places at the same time using video conferencing.
People send emails to each other from different cities.
questions.explanation: Asynchronous means 'different times' and Co-located means 'same place.' An example would be a physical bulletin board or a shared computer in an office where different shifts leave notes for each other.

questions.question: Which challenge in Groupware (Lecture 43) refers to the fact that those who do the work of entering data into a system are often not the ones who receive the direct benefits?
options: Critical Mass
Disruption of Social Customs
The Prisoner's Dilemma
The Work-Benefit Anomaly
questions.explanation: Grudin identified that groupware often fails because it requires some people (like subordinates) to do extra work (like updating calendars) for the benefit of others (like managers).

questions.question: The concept of 'Critical Mass' in CSCW (Lecture 43) states that a groupware system is only useful when:
options: The software is free to download.
Enough people use it so that the benefits of participation outweigh the costs.
The system is powered by a supercomputer.
The users are all in the same physical location.
questions.explanation: Many collaborative tools (like instant messaging) are worthless if you are the only one using them. They require a 'critical mass' of participants to become a viable communication channel.

questions.question: In the GOMS model (Lecture 6), what does the 'S' stand for, and what is its role in task analysis?
options: Software; it refers to the operating system used.
Selection rules; they represent the user's decision-making logic when multiple methods are available.
Syntax; it refers to the programming rules of the interface.
Sensors; they represent the hardware used to track user movement.
questions.explanation: GOMS stands for Goals, Operators, Methods, and Selection rules. Selection rules are the 'if-then' logic users apply to choose a specific method for a task (e.g., 'If the hand is on the mouse, use the menu; if on the keyboard, use a shortcut').

questions.question: According to Lecture 7, 'Visual Acuity' is highest in which part of the human eye?
options: The blind spot.
The peripheral retina.
The fovea.
The optic nerve.
questions.explanation: The fovea is a small area in the center of the retina where cone cells are most densely packed, providing the highest resolution and color sensitivity. Visual acuity decreases rapidly toward the periphery.

questions.question: Lecture 8 discusses 'Stereopsis.' This term refers to the human ability to:
options: See a wide range of colors simultaneously.
Perceive depth based on the slightly different images received by each eye.
Hear sounds from multiple directions at once.
Focus on small text for long periods without strain.
questions.explanation: Stereopsis is the process in visual perception leading to the perception of the depth or three-dimensional structure of a scene from two slightly different projections on the retinas of the two eyes.

questions.question: In the Revised Memory Model (Lecture 9), what is the primary difference between 'Episodic' and 'Semantic' memory?
options: Episodic memory stores facts; Semantic memory stores emotions.
Episodic memory stores specific personal experiences; Semantic memory stores general knowledge and facts.
Episodic memory is short-term; Semantic memory is long-term.
Episodic memory involves movement; Semantic memory involves sight.
questions.explanation: Episodic memory represents our memory of events and experiences in a serial form. Semantic memory is a structured record of facts, concepts, and skills acquired throughout life.

questions.question: According to the 'Model Human Processor' (Lecture 6), the total time for a simple reaction task is calculated by adding the cycle times of which three components?
options: Input, Processing, and Output.
Perceptual, Cognitive, and Motor processors.
Keyboard, CPU, and Monitor.
Short-term, Long-term, and Sensory memory.
questions.explanation: The Model Human Processor (MHP) calculates reaction time as the sum of the cycle times of the Perceptual processor (sensing), Cognitive processor (thinking/deciding), and Motor processor (acting).

questions.question: According to the 'Model Human Processor', 'Working Memory' is often compared to a 'Scratchpad' because:
options: It is used to store permanent facts like a user's name.
It holds information temporarily while it is being actively processed or manipulated.
It is physical hardware that can be replaced easily.
It is only active when the user is using a pen-based input device.
questions.explanation: Working memory (a part of Short-term Memory) is where the 'current' information being used for a task is held. It has limited capacity and information decays if not rehearsed.

questions.question: In Lecture 9, 'Attention' is described as being 'Selective.' In the context of HCI, this means designers should:
options: Provide as much information as possible to keep the user engaged.
Use visual cues like color or bold text to guide the user's focus to important information.
Make all interface elements identical to avoid distracting the user.
Avoid using any colors other than black and white.
questions.explanation: Because human attention is limited and selective, designers must use salient cues (like contrast, color, or movement) to ensure the user notices critical system information amid clutter.

questions.question: According to Lecture 10, 'Analogical Reasoning' in problem solving involves:
options: Using mathematical formulas to find a single correct answer.
Applying knowledge from a familiar domain to a new, unfamiliar one.
Trial and error without any prior knowledge.
Memorizing a list of commands without understanding their function.
questions.explanation: Analogical reasoning allows users to map their existing knowledge of one system (e.g., an office desk) onto a new system (e.g., a computer desktop) to understand how to use it.

questions.question: In Color Theory (Lecture 8), what are 'Complementary Colors'?
options: Colors that look exactly the same.
Colors that are opposite each other on the color wheel and provide maximum contrast.
Colors that are next to each other on the color wheel.
Colors that have no brightness value.
questions.explanation: Complementary colors (like Red/Green or Blue/Yellow) sit opposite each other on the color wheel. Using them together creates high contrast, which is useful for highlighting but can cause 'visual vibration' if overused.

questions.question: Which cognitive framework mentioned in Lecture 6 focuses on how information is processed across people and artifacts rather than just inside an individual's head?
options: External Cognition
Distributed Cognition
GOMS
Model Human Processor
questions.explanation: Distributed Cognition (DCog) views a system as a collection of people and tools working together to process information, rather than focusing solely on individual internal mental states.

questions.question: In Lecture 1, the concept of 'Software Apartheid' refers to:
options: The high cost of purchasing original software licenses.
The exclusion of certain groups of people from the benefits of technology due to poor design or lack of access.
The separation of front-end and back-end developers in a software house.
A security protocol that separates user data from system files.
questions.explanation: Software Apartheid occurs when technology is designed in a way that creates a digital divide, often unintentionally excluding the elderly, people with disabilities, or those without technical training from participating in the 'Information Age.' Option B is the correct social-technical definition.

questions.question: According to Lecture 2, why is 'Techno-rage' becoming a significant issue in modern society?
options: Because computer hardware is becoming physically heavier to carry.
Because users feel a sense of helplessness and frustration when complex technology fails to work as expected.
Because the cost of internet data is rising globally.
Because video games are becoming too violent for younger players.
questions.explanation: Techno-rage is the result of 'bad design' meeting high user expectations. When an interface is non-intuitive and the user cannot achieve a simple goal, it leads to extreme frustration. Option B is the correct psychological outcome of poor HCI.

questions.question: According to Lecture 3, what is the 'Industry in Denial' regarding?
options: The fact that hardware prices are dropping too fast.
The fact that software is often too difficult for the average person to use despite marketing claims.
The fact that artificial intelligence will eventually replace all programmers.
The fact that mobile phones are replacing desktop computers.
questions.explanation: The handouts argue that many software companies prioritize adding features over improving usability, often blaming the 'stupid user' for failures rather than acknowledging their own poor design. Option B is the core of this critique.

questions.question: In Lecture 4, the 'Cost of Bad Design' includes 'Indirect Costs.' Which of the following is an example of an indirect cost?
options: The salary of the software developer.
Loss of customer loyalty and trust due to a frustrating user experience.
The price of the server hardware.
The electricity bill for the office building.
questions.explanation: While direct costs are easy to measure (like development time), indirect costs like a damaged brand reputation or the need for extensive customer support are often much higher in the long run. Option B is a classic indirect cost of poor HCI.

questions.question: According to the handouts, what is the 'New Economy' (Lecture 3) most dependent on?
options: The manufacturing of heavy machinery.
Information and the ability of people to access and use it effectively.
The discovery of new oil reserves.
The use of manual labor in agriculture.
questions.explanation: The 'New Economy' is an Information Economy. Success is no longer just about 'computing power' but about how well information is presented and manipulated by humans (HCI). Option B is the correct economic shift.

questions.question: In Lecture 15, 'Speech Synthesis' (Text-to-Speech) is described as particularly useful for which group of users?
options: Users who have forgotten their passwords.
Users with visual impairments or those in hands-busy/eyes-busy environments.
Users who do not have a physical keyboard connected.
Users who are trying to save electricity.
questions.explanation: Speech synthesis provides an alternative output channel. It is essential for accessibility (screen readers for the blind) and for tasks where the user cannot look at a screen, such as driving a car. Option B is the correct user-centered application.

questions.question: In the Star Model of the HCI Lifecycle (Lecture 26), which activity is located at the center, signifying its constant involvement in every stage?
options: Requirements Specification
Prototyping
Evaluation
Implementation
questions.explanation: Unlike the traditional Waterfall model, the Star Model is 'evaluation-centric.' This means that no matter which stage you are in (Task Analysis, Prototyping, etc.), you must pass through the central Evaluation hub to ensure the design meets user needs before moving to the next phase.
